Party_Details.Name: Lim Kok Leong

Facts.Text: From 1990 to 1993, Lim Kok Leong worked as a salesperson for the partnership business that later became Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
In 1993, Lim Kok Leong joined HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd as managing director.
In 1996, Lim Kok Leong received 10,000 shares in Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd from the fourth defendant without payment, in recognition of his work for the prior partnership business.
In 1996, Lim Kok Leong was appointed as a director of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not participate in most directors' meetings of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not attend the annual general meetings of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not request access to the accounting or other records of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd until July 2014.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not undertake financial liabilities on behalf of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and did not sign official documents on its behalf.
On 16 January 2003, Lim Kok Leong became a director of Acmetec Corporation Pte Ltd.
On 3 August 2004, Lim Kok Leong ceased to be a director of Acmetec Corporation Pte Ltd.
From 1989 to 1991, Lim Kok Leong operated Tricess Enterprise as a sole proprietorship.
On 1 July 2014, Lim Kok Leong requested inspection of the accounting and other records of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd for the last five years.
After 1 July 2014, further similar requests by Lim Kok Leong to inspect the accounting and other records of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd were not granted.
On 7 July 2014, Lim Kok Leong initiated court proceedings seeking an order for inspection of the accounting and other records of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
Around 26 July 2014, Lim Kok Leong became aware that he had been removed as a director of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director of a company is entitled to inspect the company’s accounting and other records under section 199(3) of the Companies Act, and whether that right can be enforced against both the company and its directors.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 199 of the Companies Act, a company and its directors and managers must keep accounting and other records that explain the company’s transactions and financial position, and those records must be kept so they are open to inspection by directors at all times. At common law, a director has a right to inspect the company’s accounting and other records to enable performance of director functions, and this right is generally exercisable without the need to show a specific reason, unless the inspection is sought for an improper purpose that would be detrimental to the company.
Party_Details.Application: Lim Kok Leong was appointed a director of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd in 1996 and remained a director at the time he requested access to the company’s accounting and other records in July 2014. He had not previously requested inspection of those records and had limited involvement in management activities, but section 199(3) refers generally to directors without distinguishing between levels of activity. The company and its directors were responsible for keeping the accounting and other records and for making them available for inspection by directors. The defendants pointed to Lim Kok Leong’s past roles in Acmetec Corporation Pte Ltd and Tricess Enterprise and to the timing of his request after his removal as a director of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd, but these events occurred years earlier and did not, on their face, inspection of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd’s records would necessarily be used in a way that harms the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge characterised section 199(1) of the Companies Act as imposing a statutory obligation on the company and its officers to keep and maintain accounting and other records, with penal sanctions for non-compliance. Section 199(3) was interpreted as creating a mandatory obligation that the company’s records be open to inspection by directors at all times, and prior authorities such as Wuu Khek Chiang George v ECRC Land Pte Ltd were cited for the proposition that a director’s right of inspection is described as absolute. The judgment explained that at common law a director’s right of inspection is a legal incident of directorship, linked to fiduciary duties, and that section 199 recognises rather than diminishes this right. The judge rejected the defendants’ argument that Lim Kok Leong’s status as a sleeping director or his prior lack of interest in the company’s affairs limited his inspection right, relying on authorities that do not distinguish between categories of directors for this purpose. The court adopted a wide view of the purpose of inspection, including enabling directors to discharge statutory and general duties, and held that the right is not subject to a residual discretion of the court but may be denied only where inspection is sought for an improper purpose or to injure the company. The burden was placed on the defendants to show clear proof of improper purpose or likely substantial harm to the company, and the judge found that the defendants’ reliance on Lim Kok Leong’s past involvement in Acmetec and Tricess, and on the timing of his request after his removal as a director of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd, did not satisfy this threshold. The allegations of intended detriment or breach of fiduciary duty were described as bare and unmeritorious. On the joinder issue, the judge analysed section 199 in comparison with section 147 of the UK Companies Act 1948 and section 161A of the Victorian Companies Act 1961, concluding that the Singapore provision extends the duty to keep records to directors and managers personally. From this, the judge inferred that Parliament intended to allow an aggrieved director to obtain an order compelling not only the company but also fellow directors to produce the records, and rejected the defendants’ reliance on Conway v Petronius Clothing Co Ltd as authority against joining directors. The application for inspection was therefore granted, with conditions on confidentiality, cost allocation for retrieval and transport of documents, and a specified inspection period, and costs of the application were ordered against the defendants.

Party_Details.Name: Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and others

Facts.Text: Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1996 as a company engaged in general wholesale business, including trading in electronic components, electrical cables, network cables, and accessories.
Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd operated as a family-run business.
Before the incorporation of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd in 1996, the business operated as a partnership founded by the fourth defendant and the third defendant, who are husband and wife.
During the partnership period before 1996, the fourth defendant led and managed the partnership business that later became Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1993, and the second, third, and fifth defendants were shareholders of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd.
Around 1993, the second, third, and fifth defendants, as shareholders of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd, were involved in appointing Lim Kok Leong as managing director of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd.
On 1 July 2014, Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and its directors did not grant Lim Kok Leong’s request to inspect the accounting and other records of the company for the last five years.
After 1 July 2014, Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and its directors did not grant subsequent similar requests by Lim Kok Leong to inspect the company’s accounting and other records.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and its directors were provide Lim Kok Leong, as a director, access to the company’s accounting and other records under section 199(3) of the Companies Act, and whether such an obligation was enforceable against both the company and its directors.
Party_Details.Rule: Section 199 of the Companies Act requires a company and its directors and managers to keep accounting and other records that explain the company’s transactions and financial position, and to keep those records in a manner that allows convenient and proper auditing. The provision states that these records must be kept at the registered office or another place chosen by the directors and must at all times be open to inspection by directors. Case law interprets this as giving directors a right of inspection that is generally not a requirement to show specific reasons, but the right may be restricted if inspection is sought for an improper purpose that would be detrimental to the company, with the burden on those opposing inspection to show such improper purpose.
Party_Details.Application: Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd, as a company incorporated in 1996, and its directors were responsible for keeping accounting and other records and for making them available for inspection by directors. When Lim Kok Leong requested inspection of the records in July 2014, the company and its directors did not provide access. The defendants argued that the inspection right arose only against the company and that directors should not be joined personally, and they referred to the plaintiff’s long period of non-involvement and to his roles in Acmetec Corporation Pte Ltd and Tricess Enterprise, as well as the timing of his request after his removal as a director of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd. The court examined section 199’s wording, which places the duty to keep records on both the company and its directors and managers, and considered comparative statutory provisions and authorities to determine whether an order for inspection could be directed at both the company and its directors and whether the defendants had shown that the requested inspection would be used for an improper purpose.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge held that section 199(1) of the Companies Act imposes obligations on both the company and its directors and managers to keep and maintain accounting and other records, and that section 199(3) requires that these records be open to inspection by directors at all times. Relying on Wuu Khek Chiang George v ECRC Land Pte Ltd and related authorities, the court treated the director’s right of inspection as effectively absolute, subject only to denial where inspection is sought for an improper purpose or to injure the company. The judge concluded that the defendants, who opposed inspection, bore the burden of proving improper purpose and that they had not provided clear proof that granting inspection would be detrimental to the company or that substantial harm would result. The defendants’ reliance on Lim Kok Leong’s past involvement in Acmetec Corporation Pte Ltd and Tricess Enterprise, and on the timing of his request after his removal as a director of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd, was found insufficient to establish mala fides or an ulterior purpose. On the question of whether the plaintiff could proceed against the directors personally, the judge compared section 199 with section 147 of the UK Companies Act 1948 and section 161A of the Victorian Companies Act 1961, noting that the foreign provisions placed the duty to keep accounts on the company alone, whereas section 199(1) expressly extends the duty to directors and managers. From this difference, and from a holistic reading of section 199 in light of the common law right of inspection, the judge inferred that Parliament intended to allow a director to obtain an inspection order enforceable against both the company and its fellow directors. The court rejected the defendants’ interpretation of Conway v Petronius Clothing Co Ltd as precluding joinder of directors, explaining that Conway addressed the nature of the right and the court’s discretion rather than the propriety of joining directors as parties. The judge therefore declined to dismiss the action against the second to sixth defendants and ordered that the company and its directors provide access to the records, subject to conditions on confidentiality, cost allocation, and the time frame for inspection, and awarded fixed costs against the defendants.
